narrative:
As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've built a solid academic foundation with an undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 42, which stands out compared to the usual 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, and come from an upper-middle-class family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 62.6%. This high probability tipped the decision against your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away to maintain their strong bar passage rates.

The model's judgment hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the top influence pushing toward a failure prediction. Despite being above average, it wasn't quite strong enough in the model's eyes to signal bar exam readiness on the first attempt, making it the single biggest drag on your outcome. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 9—well above the average 5.53—which worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass.

Your family income bracket, upper-middle, ranked third and nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, perhaps because the model associates it with certain performance patterns seen in past students. Overall, these elements outweighed your impressive LSAT and other strengths, placing you just outside the profile of typical admitted students who show even tighter alignment across grades and background for bar success. It's a tough break, especially given your clear potential as a dedicated full-time student from a Hispanic background where representation in law matters deeply.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher or bolstering your GPA narrative with explanations of any challenges in later school years. Gathering strong letters from professors who can vouch for your Year 3 improvement could also help in appeals or reapplying next cycle. Many students in similar spots refine their profiles and succeed—focus on targeted improvements, and you'll boost your odds significantly.
